Been there 8x in 3 years got remarried there too. Life is much easier if you choose to stay in the country but capital life can be hectic. 

I love the country,,still so undeveloped and almost everyone knows each other in each village. Very carefree.  They may not have much but they have time and each other's and they gets to each breakfast and dinner together every evening.

comments.author: @NguoiVietSongXaQue
comments.text: This is a masterpiece. The beautiful cinematography is perfectly complemented by a stellar choice of music. The audio-visual harmony creates an emotional depth that is rarely seen on YouTube nowadays.

comments.author: @vincemcgough8124
comments.text: Is there anyone who thinks this narrator is actually a real person, and is there anyone who believes that if this narrator is a real human being, did they actually go to these places highlighted in the video?  There are plenty of “general” comments that are not actually “wrong”,  (they go to work, they socialize, etc etc), but this is just AI harvesting notes from books, or if human, reading other people’s notes about Asia, and pirating photos from the internet. Unfortunately AI lies to the public, and lazy reporters do no investigating themselves.  They don’t even match up the photos they use with accurate info.

comments.author: @dirkboon100
comments.text: Now living almost 5 years like this in a little village in the jungle. Wildlife all around.. I come from a European big city and the spiritual rest of this life is beautiful. Fishing in the river, markets and in the evening the bbq outside in a big mosquito tent I build from bamboo. A motorcycle like everybody else and a hard working thai wife. Fruit in my garden. Its actually a paradise.

replies.author: @LordDeplored
replies.text:
I'd like to live in rural SE Asia, especially Laos, but the air pollution is gnarly, especially in Laos.
Probably have to settle for coastal Cambodia but apparently that's pretty overdeveloped.
It's a shame because Laos seems more my style but the burning season seems brutal for folks with respiratory issues like me.

comments.text: Lao PDR is not a kingdom. It used to rely on rivers for transportation, but today hydro electricity is generated by many dams. There is an extensive road system and a Chinese high-speed railway linking northern Lao and Vientiane the capital.

comments.author: @1peik
comments.text: Response to Colin Swift : your criticism of the monks not being allowed to grow their own food as being stupid is way out of line.   The Loas people have their own culture & strong Buddhist Faith.  Feeding  and providing for the monks,  is the Buddhism belief in  gaining merit in the next Eternal World/ Karma.  If one does not have something nice to say, it is best to keep one’s “ gob( mouth)” 😮shut.

comments.author: @aurorarussell3062
comments.text:
Figuratively speaking, Laos is a crescendo of all things peaceful; the epitome of tranquility. 
In spite of wars and struggles, she maintains her composure in quiet beauty.
Laotians are loyal, humbled, and graceful. 
Maintain your elegance and dignity. 
Don’t let anything or anyone interpret those good qualities as weaknesses and take advantage of your virtues.
No, I am not a Laotian, but I am an observer from the other side of the earth.
